title: Focaccia with herbs and sea salt
subtitle: Italian flatbread with olive oil, rosemary, and flaky salt
excerpt: A high-hydration Italian flatbread dimpled with olive oil and scattered with fresh rosemary and flaky sea salt. Crisp on the outside, open-crumbed within. Makes one tray.
sourceNotes: Focaccia is documented in Italian household and bakery records from the Ligurian coast from at least the medieval period. The olive oil and sea salt form is the simplest and oldest, recorded in Italian household manuals predating the twentieth century. The high-hydration dough dimpled before baking and finished with herbs and salt is consistent across Ligurian records. The proportions recorded here follow the standard Ligurian form rather than later variations with toppings.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled, wrapped. Reheat from frozen at 200°C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles into a full-size roasting tin. Increase bake time by 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After the first rise, the dough can be refrigerated overnight. Bring to room temperature for 1 hour before dimpling and baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 230°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Pressing the fingertips firmly into a focaccia dough just before baking to create the characteristic crater surface. The dimples hold pools of olive oil during baking, creating crisp pockets on the surface while the interior stays soft. Press all the way down to the tray — shallow dimples fill in during baking.

content.text: Focaccia is a wet dough. It should feel loose and sticky in the bowl and should not be worked like a normal bread dough. The olive oil in the tin is generous — do not reduce it. The base fries in the oil as the bread bakes and that is where the crispness comes from.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{yeast-dried}} in a large bowl. Add 400 ml warm water and 60 ml of the {{olive-oil}} and mix to a rough, wet dough. No kneading: just mix until no dry flour remains. Cover and leave at room temperature for 90 minutes. The dough should roughly double and look puffy.

Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. Pour the remaining {{olive-oil}} into a 30 × 20 cm baking tin and tilt to coat the base generously. Tip the dough into the tin and stretch it gently to fill the edges. Leave for 20 minutes.

the surface all over with your fingertips, pressing firmly to the base. Scatter with fresh rosemary leaves and a generous pinch of flaky sea salt. Bake for 22–25 minutes, until deep gold on the top and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes before cutting.
